1: The festival offering calendar of Numbers 28-29 begins a new legislative unit addressing the moadîm — appointed times. These chapters supplement the calendar of Lev 23 with precise offering quantities that Leviticus does not specify. The placement of the calendar legislation here — after the census and before the Transjordanian campaigns — may reflect a preparation for settled land-worship after the conquest, or may be the narrative completion of the Sinai legislation that began in Leviticus.
2: ʾet-qorḇānî laḥmî ləʾiššay rêaḥ nîḥōḥî — my offering, the food of my fire offerings, a pleasing aroma to me. The language frames the sacrificial system as YHWH's provision for his own communal meals with Israel — an ANE idiom where deity-worship involved the feeding of the god. The P tradition uses this language while simultaneously affirming that YHWH does not literally eat (Psalms 50:12-13); the fire offering as pleasing aroma is a relational/acceptance metaphor, not a literal feeding. The command is stated as personal divine preference: observe at its appointed time.
3: The תָּמִיד (tāmîḏ) — the regular/perpetual offering — two yearling male lambs every day. The tamid became the institutional heartbeat of both Tabernacle and Temple; the Mishnah tractate Tamid describes the Second Temple's daily offering in minute detail across 7 chapters. In the post-Temple era, the morning and afternoon prayer services (Shacharit and Minchah) were understood to replace the tamid as the daily acts of covenant maintenance.
4: One lamb in the morning (babbōqer) and one at twilight (bên hāʿarbayyim — between the two evenings). The two-part daily offering structurally sanctifies the entire day: morning and evening frames enclose all human activity within the covenant acknowledgment. The Passover lamb was slaughtered at the same twilight time (Exodus 12:6), connecting the daily maintenance offering to the founding redemptive sacrifice.
5: The grain accompaniment: one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil — matching the specifications of Numbers 15:4 for the lamb-sized grain offering. The consistency with Numbers 15's graduated offering system integrates the tamid into the broader sacrificial framework. The tamid represents the minimum standard of daily covenant maintenance; all other offerings are additions to this baseline.
6: The tamid is characterized as ʿōlat tāmîḏ — the regular/perpetual burnt offering — established at Sinai (hāʿăśûyâ bəhar Sînay). The Sinai institution establishes the tamid as a permanent covenant obligation, not a later innovation. The phrase gives the daily offering its maximum authoritative grounding: it originated at the foundational covenant-moment and reflects YHWH's ongoing claim on each day of Israel's life.
7: The drink offering (nesek) for the tamid: one-fourth of a hin of fermented drink (šēkār). The specification of šēkār — strong drink, likely barley beer — rather than wine for the daily offering is unusual; most other offerings specify wine. This may reflect the availability of barley beer as the ordinary daily beverage in the ancient Near East versus wine as more festive; the tamid uses what is daily rather than what is special.
8: The evening lamb receives the same grain and drink offering as the morning lamb. The symmetry of morning and evening — identical sacrifices framing the day — creates a liturgical bracket: Israel begins and ends each day with the same act of covenant acknowledgment, making the entire day a continuous act of worship.
9: The Sabbath offering doubles the daily: two lambs plus a double grain offering. The Sabbath receives exactly twice the regular weekday offering, expressing the Sabbath's qualitative distinction through quantitative intensification. The doubled offering does not replace the tamid (v10) but supplements it; on the Sabbath, Israel brings both sets of offerings.
10: The Sabbath offering is in addition to (ʿal) the regular daily tamid and its drink offering. The additive structure of the festival calendar — each higher-level offering builds on rather than replaces the lower — creates a hierarchy of covenant intensity: daily offering + Sabbath addition + new moon addition + festival addition. The total offering burden increases with the occasion's significance.
11: The רֹאשׁ חֹדֶשׁ (rōʾš ḥōḏeš) — New Moon — offering: two young bulls, one ram, seven yearling male lambs. The lunar calendar governed the Israelite festival schedule; the new moon was both a calendar-reset marker and a monthly covenant celebration. New moon observance appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as a significant worship occasion (1 Samuel 20:5,18,24-27; Isaiah 1:13-14; Amos 8:5; Hosea 2:11), though not among the three pilgrimage festivals. The new moon offering is the most elaborate between the daily and the festival offerings.
12: The graduated grain offering quantities for the new moon follow the bull-ram-lamb scale established in Num 15: three-tenths for each bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistent scaling across all occasions (daily, Sabbath, new moon, festivals) reflects the P tradition's unified sacrificial mathematics — the same proportional system applies regardless of the occasion's scale.
13: One-tenth of an ephah for each of the seven lambs at the new moon. The seven lambs — the number of completeness — in the new moon offering mirrors the seven lambs in the Sukkot offering (29:13ff) and in the Passover offering (v19), suggesting a structural connection between the monthly renewal and the major annual festivals.
14: The drink offerings at the new moon are specified with particular precision: half a hin of wine per bull, one-third of a hin per ram, one-quarter per lamb. The increase over the daily tamid quantities (one-quarter per lamb, v7) signals the new moon's elevated status. The total new moon drink offering exceeds the daily by a substantial amount, making wine consumption at the new moon a significant community event.
15: One male goat (śəʿîr ʿizzîm ʾeḥāḏ) as a sin offering at the new moon — the same sin-offering goat that appears at every festival throughout Numbers 28-29. The monthly sin offering cleanses the accumulated unintentional errors of the past month, providing a regular mechanism for communal purification between the major annual atoning events. The new moon became a minor but regular atonement moment in Israel's ritual calendar.
16: Passover (14th of the first month) is noted briefly as a single-verse marker — the instructions are already in Exod 12 and Leviticus 23:5. Chs 28-29 focus on the offering quantities, not the ceremony; Passover's ceremony is legislated elsewhere, while Numbers specifies only the sacrificial complement. The 14th day is the day of slaughter; vv17-25 cover the seven-day Unleavened Bread festival that follows.
17: The Festival of Unleavened Bread (Chag HaMatzot) begins on 15th Nisan and runs seven days. The combination of Passover (14th) and Unleavened Bread (15th-21st) creates an eight-day spring festival complex that Leviticus and Deuteronomy treat as a single integrated event. The removal of leaven from Israelite homes for seven days was a complete domestic purge — all traces of the old fermentation removed, signaling both the hasty departure from Egypt and the beginning of a new season.
18: The first day of Unleavened Bread is a miqrāʾ-qōḏeš — a holy assembly/convocation. The term מִקְרָא קֹדֶשׁ (miqrāʾ-qōḏeš) — literally a sacred calling — designates a day on which normal labor is prohibited and the community assembles for worship. Six of Israel's annual occasions carry this status: the first and seventh days of Unleavened Bread, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Shemini Atzeret (Numbers 29:35).
19: The Unleavened Bread festival offering: two bulls, one ram, seven lambs — identical to the Shavuot offering (vv27), the Rosh Hashanah offering (Numbers 29:2), and the Yom Kippur offering (Numbers 29:8). The numerical uniformity of these four major offerings may indicate a common sacrificial template for major holy assemblies, with Sukkot's escalating bulls (Numbers 29:12-38) representing a separate, more elaborate pattern.
20: The grain offerings follow the standard graduated scale: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram. The festival grain offerings are substantially larger than the daily tamid's one-tenth, reflecting the festival's greater intensity of covenant acknowledgment. The precision of quantities across all festivals ensures that the calendar's sacrificial requirements are unambiguous and consistent.
21: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of the Unleavened Bread offering. The seven lambs appearing consistently across major festivals (Unleavened Bread, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, Sukkot) create a numerical signature for covenant-celebration occasions — the number of completeness applied to the flock-offering as the standard festive sacrificial unit.
22: One male goat as sin offering during Unleavened Bread — the same sin-offering structure as the new moon and all major festivals. The goat sin offering provides the atoning dimension for each festival occasion: celebration of covenant blessing is paired with acknowledgment of covenant failure, ensuring that festival worship does not bypass the community's ongoing need for atonement.
23: All festival offerings are in addition to (milelāḏ ʿōlat habbōqer) the regular morning tamid. The additive structure means that on high-festival days the total offering burden was substantial — the daily tamid + Sabbath addition (if applicable) + festival offering all accumulate. This layering prevents festivals from replacing daily worship; they intensify the covenant relationship while the tamid maintains its continuous baseline.
24: The same pattern of food-offering repeats each of the seven days of Unleavened Bread. The seven-day repetition builds the festival's duration into the sacrificial calendar: the Exodus is not commemorated in a single day's offering but across a full week's repeated acts of covenant renewal. The repetition embodies the Unleavened Bread's theological point: the separation from Egypt's leaven is not a moment but a sustained orientation.
25: The seventh day of Unleavened Bread is also a holy assembly with no regular work — mirroring the first day (v18). The festival's boundaries are marked by matched holy assemblies, giving the entire week the character of a sacred bracket: enter with holiness, travel through seven days of offering, exit with holiness. The seventh-day holy assembly also recalls the Sabbath structure: seven days leading to a sacred culmination.
26: The Festival of Firstfruits — Yôm habbikkûrîm, identified here as the Day of Firstfruits — is what Exodus 23:16 calls the Festival of Weeks (Shavuot) and Deuteronomy 16:9-12 connects to counting seven weeks from the first harvest. The name bikkûrîm — firstfruits — frames Shavuot primarily as an agricultural thanksgiving: the first grain of the new harvest is presented to YHWH. The Septuagint calls it Pentecost (fiftieth day), reflecting the counting tradition of Leviticus 23:15-16.
27: The Shavuot offering: two bulls, one ram, seven lambs — identical to the Passover festival offering. The structural parallel between Passover and Shavuot in the Numbers calendar connects the spring grain festival to the spring exodus festival as components of a single covenant season. In Second Temple and early Christian interpretation, Shavuot at Sinai (when Torah was given) was linked to Shavuot in Acts 2 (when the Spirit was given) as fulfillment-of-type.
28: The grain offerings for Shavuot follow the same graduated scale as all major festivals: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistency of the offering math across all major festivals creates a unified sacrificial language; each festival speaks with the same numerical vocabulary while varying in duration, season, and ceremony.
29: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of Shavuot. The seven-lamb signature applies here as throughout the festival calendar. The precision of one-tenth per lamb (rather than a total grain offering for all seven) reflects the individual accountability built into the offering system: each animal has its specified accompaniment.
30: One male goat for Shavuot's sin offering — the ubiquitous festival atonement offering. The goat sin offering's presence at every major festival (new moon, Passover week, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, each day of Sukkot) makes it the structural atonement companion to all festive celebration. No major occasion in Israel's calendar passes without an accompanying acknowledgment of sin and its need for divine forgiveness.
31: All Shavuot offerings are in addition to the regular tamid. The closing additive formula (milelāḏ ʿōlat hattāmîḏ) closes the Shavuot section as it closed the Unleavened Bread section (v23). The reiteration of additive structure at each unit's close ensures that the layered system is not misread as replacement; the tamid persists beneath all festival observance as the daily covenant foundation.
